An **armoured fighting vehicle** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")) or **armored fighting vehicle** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")) (**AFV**) is an armed [combat vehicle](/wiki/Combat_vehicle "Combat vehicle") protected by [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour"), generally combining [operational mobility](/wiki/Operational_mobility "Operational mobility") with [offensive](/wiki/Offensive_(military) "Offensive (military)") and [defensive](/wiki/Defense_(military) "Defense (military)") capabilities. AFVs can be wheeled or [tracked](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track"). Examples of AFVs are [tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"), [armoured cars](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)"), [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun"), [self-propelled artilleries](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery"), [infantry fighting vehicles](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle") (IFV), and [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") (APC).

Armoured fighting vehicles are classified according to their characteristics and intended role on the battlefield. The classifications are not absolute; two countries may classify the same vehicle differently, and the criteria change over time. For example, relatively lightly armed armoured personnel carriers were largely superseded by infantry fighting vehicles with much heavier armament in a similar role.

Successful designs are often adapted to a wide variety of applications. For example, the [MOWAG Piranha](/wiki/MOWAG_Piranha "MOWAG Piranha"), originally designed as an APC, has been adapted to fill numerous roles such as a [mortar carrier](/wiki/Mortar_carrier "Mortar carrier"), infantry fighting vehicle, and assault gun.

Armoured fighting vehicles began to appear in use in World War I with the armoured car, the tank, the self-propelled gun, and the personnel carrier seeing use. By World War II, armies had large numbers of AFVs, together with other vehicles to carry troops this permitted highly mobile [manoeuvre warfare](/wiki/Maneuver_warfare "Maneuver warfare").

## Evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Evolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Schlacht_bei_Zama_Gem%C3%A4lde_H_P_Motte.jpg/250px-Schlacht_bei_Zama_Gem%C3%A4lde_H_P_Motte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schlacht_bei_Zama_Gem%C3%A4lde_H_P_Motte.jpg)

[Battle of Zama](/wiki/Battle_of_Zama "Battle of Zama") by [Henri-Paul Motte](/wiki/Henri-Paul_Motte "Henri-Paul Motte"), 1890

The concept of a highly mobile and protected fighting unit has been around for centuries; from [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal")'s [war elephants](/wiki/War_elephant "War elephant") to [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s [contraptions](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo's fighting vehicle"), military strategists endeavoured to maximize the mobility and survivability of their soldiers.

Armoured fighting vehicles were not possible until [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine") of sufficient power became available at the start of the 20th century.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Helepolis.png/250px-Helepolis.png)](/wiki/File:Helepolis.png)

A [Helepolis](/wiki/Helepolis "Helepolis")-like Siege Engine showing ballistae, stairs and movement capstan

Modern armoured fighting vehicles represent the realization of an ancient concept – that of providing troops with mobile protection and firepower. Armies have deployed war machines and cavalries with rudimentary armour in battle for millennia. Use of these animals and engineering designs sought to achieve a balance between the conflicting needs of mobility, firepower and protection.

##### Siege machine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Siege machine")]

[Siege engines](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine"), such as [battering rams](/wiki/Battering_ram "Battering ram") and [siege towers](/wiki/Siege_tower "Siege tower"), would often be armoured in order to protect their crews from enemy action. [Polyidus of Thessaly](/wiki/Polyidus_of_Thessaly "Polyidus of Thessaly") developed a very large movable siege tower, the *[helepolis](/wiki/Helepolis "Helepolis")*, as early as 340 BC, and Greek forces used such structures in the [Siege of Rhodes](/wiki/Siege_of_Rhodes_(305_BC) "Siege of Rhodes (305 BC)") (305 BC).

The idea of a protected fighting vehicle has been known since antiquity. Frequently cited is [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s 15th-century sketch of a [mobile, protected gun-platform](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo da Vinci's fighting vehicle"); the drawings show a conical, wooden shelter with apertures for cannons around the circumference. The machine was to be mounted on four wheels which would be turned by the crew through a system of [hand cranks](/wiki/Hand_crank#Hand-powered_cranks "Hand crank") and [cage (or "lantern") gears](/wiki/Gear#Cage_gear "Gear"). Leonardo claimed: "I will build armoured wagons which will be safe and invulnerable to enemy attacks. There will be no obstacle which it cannot overcome."[[1]](#cite_note-1) Modern replicas have demonstrated that the human crew would have been able to move it over only short distances.

##### War wagon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: War wagon")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Husitsky_bojovy_vuz_replika.jpg/250px-Husitsky_bojovy_vuz_replika.jpg)](/wiki/File:Husitsky_bojovy_vuz_replika.jpg)

Modern reconstruction of [Hussite](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") [war wagon](/wiki/War_wagon "War wagon")

[Hussite](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") forces in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") developed [war wagons](/wiki/War_wagon "War wagon") – [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") horse-drawn [wagons](/wiki/Wagon "Wagon") that doubled as [wagon forts](/wiki/Wagon_fort "Wagon fort") – around 1420 during the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"). These heavy wagons were given protective sides with firing slits; their heavy firepower came from either a cannon or from a force of [hand-gunners](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon") and [crossbowmen](/wiki/Crossbowmen "Crossbowmen"), supported by [light cavalry](/wiki/Light_cavalry "Light cavalry") and [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") using [pikes](/wiki/Pike_(weapon) "Pike (weapon)") and [flails](/wiki/Flail_(weapon) "Flail (weapon)"). Heavy [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") mounted on wagons were called *arquebus à croc*. These carried a ball of about 3.5 ounces (100 g).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

#### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modern")]

By the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), most modern armies had vehicles to carry infantry, artillery and [anti-aircraft weaponry](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_weapon "Anti-aircraft weapon"). Most modern AFVs are superficially similar in design to their World War II counterparts, but with significantly better armour, weapons, engines, electronics, and suspension. The increase in the capacity of [transport aircraft](/wiki/Military_transport_aircraft "Military transport aircraft") makes possible and practicable the transport of AFVs by air. Many armies are replacing some or all of their traditional heavy vehicles with lighter airmobile versions, often with wheels instead of tracks.

##### Armored road trains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Armored road trains")]

In the spring of 1900 [Lord Roberts](/wiki/Frederick_Roberts,_1st_Earl_Roberts "Frederick Roberts, 1st Earl Roberts") ordered six armored road trains for use in South Africa; in December 1901 four armored road trains were delivered after testing. Each train was pulled by a John Fowler & Co [steam tractor](/wiki/Steam_tractor "Steam tractor"); with three armored wagons that could be used to carry troops and supplies. However these engines had their armor removed after being delivered, and were used by the Imperial Military Railway.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Beavan-217-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

##### Armed and armoured car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Armed and armoured car")]

The first modern AFVs were armed cars, dating back virtually to the invention of the [motor car](/wiki/Car "Car"). The British inventor [F. R. Simms](/wiki/Frederick_Richard_Simms "Frederick Richard Simms") designed and built the [Motor Scout](/wiki/Motor_Scout "Motor Scout") in 1898. It was the first armed, petrol-engine powered vehicle ever built. It consisted of a [De Dion-Bouton](/wiki/De_Dion-Bouton "De Dion-Bouton") [quadracycle](/wiki/Quadracycle "Quadracycle") with a [Maxim machine gun](/wiki/Maxim_gun "Maxim gun") mounted on the front bar. An iron shield offered some protection for the driver from the front, but it lacked all-around protective armour.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacksey1980-7)

The [armoured car](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") was the first modern fully armoured fighting vehicle. The first of these was the [Simms's Motor War Car](/wiki/Motor_War_Car "Motor War Car"), also designed by Simms and built by [Vickers, Sons & Maxim](/wiki/Vickers#Vickers,_Sons_&_Maxim "Vickers") in 1899.[[8]](#cite_note-DCMB-8) The vehicle had Vickers armour 6 mm thick and was powered by a four-cylinder 3.3-litre[[8]](#cite_note-DCMB-8) 16 [hp](/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower") Cannstatt Daimler engine giving it a maximum speed of around 9 miles per hour (14 kilometres per hour). The armament, consisting of two Maxim guns, was carried in two turrets with 360° traverse.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacksey1980256-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Another early armoured car of the period was the French [Charron, Girardot et Voigt 1902](/wiki/Charron,_Girardot_et_Voigt_1902 "Charron, Girardot et Voigt 1902"), presented at the *Salon de l'Automobile et du cycle* in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), on 8 March 1902.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGougaud198711-11) The vehicle was equipped with a [Hotchkiss machine gun](/wiki/Hotchkiss_machine_gun "Hotchkiss machine gun"), and with 7 mm armour for the gunner.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The Spanish [Schneider-Brillié](/wiki/Schneider-Brilli%C3%A9_model_1909 "Schneider-Brillié model 1909") was the first armored vehicle to be used in combat, being first used in the [Kert Campaign](/wiki/Kert_campaign "Kert campaign"). The vehicle was equipped with two machineguns and built from a bus chassis.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Armored cars also saw limited use in the [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War").[[15]](#cite_note-BekeleDirar2018-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Armoured cars were first used in large numbers on both sides during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") as scouting vehicles.

Main article: [History of the tank](/wiki/History_of_the_tank "History of the tank")

In 1903, [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") published the short story "[The Land Ironclads](/wiki/The_Land_Ironclads "The Land Ironclads")," positing indomitable war machines that would bring a new age of land warfare, the way steam-powered [ironclad warships](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship") had ended the [age of sail](/wiki/Age_of_sail "Age of sail").

Wells's literary vision was realized in 1916, when, amidst the pyrrhic standstill of the [Great War](/wiki/Great_War "Great War"), the British [Landship Committee](/wiki/Landship_Committee "Landship Committee") deployed revolutionary armoured vehicles to break the stalemate. The tank was envisioned as an armoured machine that could cross ground under fire from [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") and reply with its own mounted machine guns and [naval artillery](/wiki/Naval_artillery "Naval artillery"). These first [British tanks of World War I](/wiki/Tanks_of_World_War_I "Tanks of World War I") moved on [caterpillar tracks](/wiki/Caterpillar_track "Caterpillar track") that had substantially lower ground pressure than wheeled vehicles, enabling them to pass the muddy, pocked terrain and slit trenches of the [Battle of the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme").

##### Troop transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Troop transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_Tank_Museum_%282012%29.jpg/250px-The_Tank_Museum_%282012%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Tank_Museum_(2012).jpg)

[Mark IX tank](/wiki/Mark_IX_tank "Mark IX tank"), the first armoured personnel carrier, at the Tank Museum, Bovington

The tank eventually proved highly successful and, as technology improved, it became a weapon that could cross large distances at much higher speeds than supporting [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"). The need to provide the units that would fight alongside the tank led to the development of a wide range of specialised AFVs, especially during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") (1939–1945).

The armoured personnel carrier, designed to transport infantry troops to the frontline, emerged towards the end of World War I. During the first actions with [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), it had become clear that close contact with infantry was essential in order to secure ground won by the tanks. Troops on foot were vulnerable to enemy fire, but they could not be transported in the tank because of the intense heat and noxious atmosphere.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1917, Lieutenant G. J. Rackham was ordered to design an armoured vehicle that could fight and carry troops or supplies. The [Mark IX tank](/wiki/Mark_IX_tank "Mark IX tank") was built by [Armstrong, Whitworth & Co.](/wiki/Armstrong_Whitworth "Armstrong Whitworth"), although just three vehicles had been finished at the time of the [Armistice](/wiki/Armistice "Armistice") in November 1918, and only 34 were built in total.

##### Tankette

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tankette")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/IJA_TK_Tankette_Display_at_Armor_School_History_Museum_20130302b.JPG/250px-IJA_TK_Tankette_Display_at_Armor_School_History_Museum_20130302b.JPG)](/wiki/File:IJA_TK_Tankette_Display_at_Armor_School_History_Museum_20130302b.JPG)

IJA [Type 94 tankette](/wiki/Type_94_tankette "Type 94 tankette") at the Armor School History Museum

Different tank classifications emerged in the interwar period. The [tankette](/wiki/Tankette "Tankette") was conceived as a mobile, two-man model, mainly intended for reconnaissance. In 1925, [Sir John Carden](/wiki/Sir_John_Carden,_6th_Baronet "Sir John Carden, 6th Baronet") and [Vivian Loyd](/wiki/Vivian_Loyd "Vivian Loyd") produced the first such design to be adopted – the [Carden Loyd tankette](/wiki/Carden_Loyd_tankette "Carden Loyd tankette"). Tankettes saw use in the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") during the [Italian invasion of Ethiopia](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") (1935–1936), the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") (1936–1939), and during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The [Imperial Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") used tankettes in [China](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") for infantry support, reconnaissance and later for [jungle warfare](/wiki/Jungle_warfare "Jungle warfare").[[17]](#cite_note-T27-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga20077,_8-19)

##### Self-propelled artillery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Self-propelled artillery")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/British_Gun_Carrier_Mark_I_-_60_pdr.jpg/250px-British_Gun_Carrier_Mark_I_-_60_pdr.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Gun_Carrier_Mark_I_-_60_pdr.jpg)

British [Gun Carrier Mark I](/wiki/Gun_Carrier_Mark_I "Gun Carrier Mark I") (60 pdr)

The British [Gun Carrier Mark I](/wiki/Gun_Carrier_Mark_I "Gun Carrier Mark I"), the first [Self-propelled artillery](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery"), was fielded in 1917. It was based on the first tank, the British [Mark I](/wiki/Mark_I_(tank) "Mark I (tank)"), and carried a heavy field-gun. The next major advance was the [Birch gun](/wiki/Birch_gun "Birch gun") (1925), developed for the British motorised warfare experimental brigade (the [Experimental Mechanized Force](/wiki/Experimental_Mechanized_Force "Experimental Mechanized Force")). This mounted a field gun, capable of the usual artillery trajectories and even anti-aircraft use, on a tank chassis.

During World War II, most major military powers developed self-propelled artillery vehicles. These had guns mounted on a tracked chassis (often that of an obsolete or superseded tank) and provided an armoured superstructure to protect the gun and its crew. The first British design, ["Bishop"](/wiki/Bishop_(artillery) "Bishop (artillery)"), carried the [25 pdr gun-howitzer](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_25_pounder "Ordnance QF 25 pounder") in an extemporised mounting on a tank chassis that severely limited the gun's performance. It was replaced by the more effective [Sexton](/wiki/Sexton_(artillery) "Sexton (artillery)"). The Germans built many lightly armoured [self-propelled anti-tank guns](/wiki/Self-propelled_anti-tank_gun "Self-propelled anti-tank gun") using captured French equipment (for example [Marder I](/wiki/Marder_I "Marder I")), their own obsolete light tank chassis ([Marder II](/wiki/Marder_II "Marder II")), or ex-Czech chassis ([Marder III](/wiki/Marder_III "Marder III")). These led to better-protected tank destroyers, built on a medium-tank chassis such as the [Jagdpanzer IV](/wiki/Jagdpanzer_IV "Jagdpanzer IV") or the [Jagdpanther](/wiki/Jagdpanther "Jagdpanther").

##### Anti-aircraft vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Anti-aircraft vehicle")]

The [Self-propelled anti-aircraft weapon](/wiki/Self-propelled_anti-aircraft_weapon "Self-propelled anti-aircraft weapon") debuted in WWI. The [German 88](/wiki/Flak_88 "Flak 88") mm[[20]](#cite_note-20) anti-aircraft gun was truck-mounted and used to great effect against British tanks, and the British [QF 3-inch 20 cwt](/wiki/QF_3-inch_20_cwt "QF 3-inch 20 cwt") was mounted on trucks for use on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)"). Although the [Birch gun](/wiki/Birch_gun "Birch gun") was a general purpose artillery piece on an armoured tracked chassis, it was capable of elevation for anti-aircraft use. [Vickers Armstrong](/wiki/Vickers_Armstrong "Vickers Armstrong") developed one of the first SPAAGs based on the chassis of the Mk.E 6-ton light tank/[Dragon Medium Mark IV tractor](/wiki/Vickers_Medium_Dragon "Vickers Medium Dragon"), mounting a Vickers QF-1 "Pom-Pom" gun of 40 mm. The Germans fielded the Sd.Kfz. 10/4 and 6/2, cargo halftracks mounting single 20 mm or 37 mm AA guns (respectively) by the start of the war.

##### Self-propelled multiple rocket-launcher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Self-propelled multiple rocket-launcher")]

Rocket launchers such as the Soviet [Katyusha](/wiki/Katyusha_rocket_launcher "Katyusha rocket launcher") originated in the late 1930s. The [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") fielded self-propelled rocket artillery in World War II – the [Panzerwerfer](/wiki/Panzerwerfer "Panzerwerfer") and [Wurfrahmen 40](/wiki/Wurfrahmen_40 "Wurfrahmen 40") equipped half-track armoured fighting vehicles. Many modern [multiple rocket launchers](/wiki/Multiple_rocket_launchers "Multiple rocket launchers") are self propelled by either truck or tank chassis.

### Iron Triangle design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Iron Triangle design")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=). *(January 2011)* |

The "**Iron Triangle**" in AFVs represents the fundamental design trade-off between protection, mobility, and firepower; enhancing one aspect usually compromises the others, requiring difficult choices for military designers balancing armor, speed, and weapon capability for a vehicle. There are no "perfect military vehicles", only carefully considered trade-offs. Newer 21st century conflicts and threats (electronic warfare, drones, advanced missiles, etc.) are introducing new elements like [C-UAS](/wiki/C-UAS "C-UAS") are stretching the triangle or creating a "**Steel Hexagon**" for modern vehicles, with maneuverability, lethality, autonomy, adaptability, connectivity, and survivability at the apexes.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

#### Survivability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Survivability")]

Main articles: [Aramid](/wiki/Aramid "Aramid"), [Ballistic glass](/wiki/Ballistic_glass "Ballistic glass"), [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron"), and [Vehicle armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour")

The level of armour protection between AFVs varies greatly – a [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") will normally be designed to take hits from other [tank guns](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") and [anti-tank missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_missiles "Anti-tank missiles"), whilst light [reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance") vehicles are often only armoured "just in case". Whilst heavier armour provides better protection, it makes vehicles less mobile (for a given engine power), limits its air-transportability, increases cost, uses more fuel and may limit the places it can go – for example, many bridges may be unable to support the weight of a main battle tank. A trend toward [composite armour](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour") is taking the place of steel – composites are stronger for a given weight, allowing the tank to be lighter for the same protection as steel armour, or better protected for the same weight. Armour is being supplemented with [active protection systems](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system") on a number of vehicles, allowing the AFV to protect itself from incoming projectiles.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/PantheraScheme.jpg/500px-PantheraScheme.jpg)](/wiki/File:PantheraScheme.jpg)

Armour-thickness chart for a [Panther tank](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank")

The level of protection also usually varies considerably throughout the individual vehicle too, depending on the role of the vehicle and the likely direction of attack. For example, a main battle tank will usually have the heaviest armour on the hull front and the turret, lighter armour on the sides of the hull and the thinnest armour on the top and bottom of the tank. Other vehicles – such as the [MRAP](/wiki/MRAP "MRAP") family – may be primarily armoured against the threat from [IEDs](/wiki/Improvised_explosive_device "Improvised explosive device") and so will have heavy, [sloped armour](/wiki/Sloped_armour "Sloped armour") on the bottom of the hull.

In modern 21st warfare, where satellites, drones, and mobile phones has all but erased the [fog of war](/wiki/Fog_of_war "Fog of war"), survivability is not about armor thickness, it's about time. The shorter an AFV's signature window, the harder they are to kill.

#### Lethality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Lethality")]

Weaponry varies by a very wide degree between AFVs – lighter vehicles for infantry carrying, reconnaissance or specialist roles may have only a [autocannon](/wiki/Autocannon "Autocannon") or [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") (or no armament at all), whereas heavy self-propelled artillery will carry [howitzers](/wiki/Howitzer "Howitzer"), [mortars](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") or [rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(weapon) "Rocket (weapon)") launchers. These weapons may be mounted on a [pintle](/wiki/Weapon_mount#Pintle "Weapon mount"), affixed directly to the vehicle or placed in a [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") or cupola.

The greater the recoil of the weapon on an AFV, the larger the turret ring needs to be. A larger turret ring necessitates a larger vehicle. To avoid listing to the side, turrets on amphibious vehicles are usually located at the centre of the vehicle.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMargiotta1996-25)

[Grenade launchers](/wiki/Grenade_launcher "Grenade launcher") provide a versatile launch platform for a plethora of munitions including, [smoke](/wiki/Smoke_grenade "Smoke grenade"), phosphorus, [tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas"), illumination, anti-personnel, infrared and radar-jamming rounds.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMargiotta1996-25)

Turret stabilization is an important capability because it enables firing on the move and prevents crew fatigue.

#### Maneuverability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Maneuverability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Auslieferung_von_Bergepanzer_2_an_die_Bundeswehr_durch_die_Kieler_Atlas-MaK_Maschinenbau_GmbH_%28Kiel_40.250%29.jpg/250px-Auslieferung_von_Bergepanzer_2_an_die_Bundeswehr_durch_die_Kieler_Atlas-MaK_Maschinenbau_GmbH_%28Kiel_40.250%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auslieferung_von_Bergepanzer_2_an_die_Bundeswehr_durch_die_Kieler_Atlas-MaK_Maschinenbau_GmbH_(Kiel_40.250).jpg)

Engine replacement for a [Bergepanzer 2](/wiki/Bergepanzer_2 "Bergepanzer 2")

Modern AFVs have primarily used either petrol (gasoline) or diesel piston engines. More recently, gas turbines have been used. Most early AFVs used [petrol engines](/wiki/Petrol_engine "Petrol engine"), as they offer a good [power-to-weight ratio](/wiki/Power-to-weight_ratio "Power-to-weight ratio"). However, they fell out of favour during World War II due to the flammability of the fuel.

Most current AFVs are powered by a [diesel engine](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine"); modern technology, including the use of [turbo-charging](/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger"), helps to overcome the lower power-to-weight ratio of diesel engines compared to petrol.

[Gas turbine](/wiki/Gas_turbine "Gas turbine") (turboshaft) engines offer a very high power-to-weight ratio and were starting to find favour in the late 20th century – however, they offer very poor fuel consumption and as such some armies are switching from gas turbines back to diesel engines (i.e. the Russian [T-80](/wiki/T-80 "T-80") used a gas turbine engine, whereas the later [T-90](/wiki/T-90 "T-90") does not). The US [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") is a notable example of a gas turbine powered tank.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Modern classification by type and role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Modern classification by type and role")]

Notable armoured fighting vehicles extending from post-World War I to today.

### Tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Tank")]

Further information: [Tank classification](/wiki/Tank_classification "Tank classification") and [Tank gun](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun")

The [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank") is an all terrain AFV incorporating artillery which is designed to fill almost all battlefield roles and to engage enemy forces by the use of [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") in the frontal assault role. Though several configurations have been tried, particularly in the early experimental "golden days" of tank development, a standard, mature design configuration has since emerged to a generally accepted pattern. This features a main [tank gun](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") or [artillery gun](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"), mounted in a fully rotating [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") atop a tracked automotive hull, with various additional secondary weapon systems throughout.

Philosophically, the tank is, by its very nature, an offensive weapon. Being a protective encasement with at least one gun position, it is essentially a [pillbox](/wiki/Pillbox_(military) "Pillbox (military)") or small [fortress](/wiki/Fortress "Fortress") (though these are static fortifications of a purely defensive nature) that can move toward the enemy – hence its offensive utility. Psychologically, the tank is a [force multiplier](/wiki/Force_multiplier "Force multiplier") that has a positive morale effect on the infantry it accompanies.[[27]](#cite_note-morale_effect-27) It also instills fear in the opposing force who can often hear and even feel their arrival.[[28]](#cite_note-McNab_Gulf_War-28)

#### Tank classifications

  [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") [T29](/wiki/T29_Heavy_Tank "T29 Heavy Tank") Heavy Tank

Classification by relative size was common, as this also tended to influence the tanks' role.

* [Light tanks](/wiki/Light_tank "Light tank") are smaller tanks with thinner armour and lower-powered guns, allowing for better tactical mobility, amphibious and airdrop options, and ease of strategic transport. These are intended for [armoured reconnaissance](/wiki/Armoured_reconnaissance "Armoured reconnaissance"), [skirmishing](/wiki/Skirmishing "Skirmishing"), [artillery observation](/wiki/Artillery_observer "Artillery observer"), [expeditionary warfare](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") and supplementing [airborne or naval landings](/wiki/Landing_operation "Landing operation"). Light tanks are typically cheaper to build and maintain, but fare poorly against heavier tanks. They may be held in reserve for exploiting any breakthroughs in enemy lines, with the goal of disrupting communications and supply lines. The light tank concept emerged during World War I with the [French Renault ft17](/wiki/Renault_FT "Renault FT").
* [Medium tanks](/wiki/Medium_tank "Medium tank") are mid-sized tanks with adequate armour and guns, and fair mobility, allowing for a balance of fighting abilities, mobility, cost-effectiveness, and transportability. Medium tanks are effective in groups when used against enemy tanks. More cost effective and easier to produce than heavier tanks they excelled in various combat roles.
* [Heavy tanks](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") are larger tanks with thick armour and more powerful guns, but less mobile and more difficult to transport. They were intended to be [overmatch](/wiki/Overmatch "Overmatch") for typical enemy medium tanks, easily penetrating their armour while being much less susceptible to their attacks. Heavy tanks cost more to both build and maintain, and their heavy armour proved most effective when deployed in support infantry assaulting entrenched fortifications. The concept of heavy tanks began in World War I with the British MK1 a large vehicle intended to breach enemy trenches.

Over time, tanks tended to be designed with heavier armour and weapons, increasing the weight of all tanks, so these classifications are relative to the average for the nation's tanks for any given period. An older tank design might be reclassified over time, such as a tank being first deployed as a medium tank, but in later years relegated to light tank roles.

Tanks were designated as [Male tank](/wiki/Male_tank "Male tank") (cannons) or [Female tank](/wiki/Female_tank "Female tank") (machine guns) based on their armament to enable them to operate in teams. Male tanks had six-pounder cannons to destroy fortifications, while female tanks were equipped with only machine guns to defend against infantry. The two types were designed to operate together, with male tanks leading to breach enemy lines and female tanks following to suppress enemy infantry. The introduction of the Hermaphrodite tank (Composite) and Whippet showed a need for tanks to handle multiple roles, leading to universal designs like the Mark VIII (International Tank), though the focus eventually shifted away from giant, slow tanks towards faster, more versatile models after WWI.

Tanks were also classified by roles that were independent of size, such as [cavalry tank](/wiki/Cavalry_tank "Cavalry tank"), [cruiser tank](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank"), [fast tank](/wiki/BT_tank "BT tank"), [infantry tank](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank"), "assault" tank, or "breakthrough" tank. Military theorists initially tended to assign tanks to traditional military infantry, cavalry, and artillery roles, but later developed more specialized roles unique to tanks.

In modern use, the heavy tank and tank destroyer has fallen out of favour, being supplanted by more heavily armed and armoured descendant of the medium tanks – the universal [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"). The light tank role has been taken over by Infantry Fighting Vehicles, MRAPS, or lost favour to cheaper, faster, lighter [armoured cars](/wiki/Armoured_car_(military) "Armoured car (military)"); however, light tanks (or similar vehicles with other names) are still in service with a number of forces as [reconnaissance vehicles](/wiki/Reconnaissance_vehicle "Reconnaissance vehicle"), most notably the [Russian Marines](/wiki/Naval_Infantry_(Russia) "Naval Infantry (Russia)") with the [PT-76](/wiki/PT-76 "PT-76"), the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") with the [Scimitar](/wiki/FV107_Scimitar "FV107 Scimitar"), and the [Chinese Army](/wiki/Chinese_Army "Chinese Army") with the [Type 63](/wiki/Type_63_(tank) "Type 63 (tank)").

#### Main battle tank

  [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") [Type 99](/wiki/Type_99_tank "Type 99 tank") main battle tank

Modern [main battle tanks](/wiki/Main_battle_tank#Main_battle_tank_(late_twentieth_century) "Main battle tank") or "universal tanks" incorporate recent advances in automotive, artillery, armour, and electronic technology to combine the best characteristics of the historic medium and heavy tanks into a single, all-around type. They are also the most expensive to mass-produce. A main battle tank is distinguished by its high level of firepower, mobility and armour protection relative to other vehicles of its era. It can cross comparatively rough terrain at high speeds, but its heavy dependency on fuel, maintenance, and ammunition makes it [logistically](/wiki/Military_logistics "Military logistics") demanding. It has the heaviest [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour") of any AFVs on the battlefield, and carries a powerful [precision-guided munition](/wiki/Precision-guided_munition "Precision-guided munition") weapon systems that may be able to engage a wide variety of both ground targets and air targets.

Up to the 1960s, countries would deploy Light and Heavy Tanks alongside their MBTs, with Heavy Tanks mounting monstrous 120mm guns and obscene armour to stay relevant. But, as the MBTs started to mount
compact but incredibly powerful guns, while gaining light, composite armour that outperformed the heavy tanks, the heavy tank classification slowly ceased to exist. The MBT did it better, but costed a lot less. The MBT also had an effect on Tank Destroyers; the classic tank destroyer died with the heavy tank, as the MBT could mount a big gun and heavy armour without sacrificing the turret, and MBTs were very capable anti-tank platforms. Despite significant advances in [anti-tank warfare](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare"), MBTs relevancy as steadily increased and still remains the most versatile and fearsome land-based weapon-systems of the 21st-century, valued for its adaptability, [shock action](/wiki/Shock_tactics "Shock tactics") and high [survivability](/wiki/Survivability "Survivability").

#### Tankette

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Tankette")]

Main article: [Tankette](/wiki/Tankette "Tankette")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Carden-Loyd_Two-Man_Tankette%2C_1926._KID235.jpg/250px-Carden-Loyd_Two-Man_Tankette%2C_1926._KID235.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carden-Loyd_Two-Man_Tankette,_1926._KID235.jpg)

The [Carden-Loyd tankette](/wiki/Carden-Loyd_tankette "Carden-Loyd tankette") concept was adopted by many armies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/W2_argus.tif/lossy-page1-250px-W2_argus.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:W2_argus.tif)

[Wiesel 2](/wiki/Wiesel_2 "Wiesel 2") *Argus* scout tankette

A tankette is a tracked armed and armoured vehicle[[29]](#cite_note-29) resembling a small "ultra-light tank" or "super-light tank" roughly the size of a car, mainly intended for light infantry support or [scouting](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Tankettes were introduced in the mid-1920s as a reconnaissance vehicle and a mobile machine gun position. They were one or two-man vehicles armed with a machine gun. Colloquially it may also simply mean a "small tank".[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Tankettes were designed and built by several nations between the 1920s and 1940s following the British [Carden Loyd tankette](/wiki/Carden_Loyd_tankette "Carden Loyd tankette") which was a successful implementation of "one man tank" ideas from [Giffard Le Quesne Martel](/wiki/Giffard_Le_Quesne_Martel "Giffard Le Quesne Martel"), a British Army engineer. They were very popular with smaller countries. Some saw some combat (with limited success) in World War II. However, the vulnerability of their light armour eventually caused the concept to be abandoned. However, the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army "German Army") uses a modern design of air-transportable armoured weapons carriers, the [Wiesel AWC](/wiki/Wiesel_AWC "Wiesel AWC"), which resembles the concept of a tankette.

#### *Super*-heavy tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Super-heavy tank")]

Main article: [Super-heavy tank](/wiki/Super-heavy_tank "Super-heavy tank")

The term "super-heavy tank" has been used to describe armoured fighting vehicles of extreme size, generally over 75 tonnes. Programs have been initiated on several occasions with the aim of creating an invincible [siegeworks](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine")/[breakthrough](/wiki/Breakthrough_(military) "Breakthrough (military)") vehicle for penetrating enemy formations and fortifications without fear of being destroyed in combat. Examples were designed in World War I and World War II (such as the [Panzer VIII Maus](/wiki/Panzer_VIII_Maus "Panzer VIII Maus")), along with a few in the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). Despite their ambitious designs remain largely experimental; few working prototypes were built and there is no clear evidence any of these vehicles saw combat, as their immense size would have made most designs impractical.

#### Missile tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Missile tank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/IT-1_missile_tank.jpg/250px-IT-1_missile_tank.jpg)](/wiki/File:IT-1_missile_tank.jpg)

Soviet [IT-1](/wiki/IT-1 "IT-1"), [Kubinka Tank Museum](/wiki/Kubinka_Tank_Museum "Kubinka Tank Museum")

A [missile tank](/wiki/Missile_tank "Missile tank") is a tank fulfilling the role of a main battle tank, but using only [anti-tank](/wiki/Anti-tank_missile "Anti-tank missile") [surface-to-surface missiles](/wiki/Surface-to-surface_missile "Surface-to-surface missile") for main armament.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Several nations have experimented with prototypes, notably the Soviet Union during the tenure of Nikita Khrushchev (projects [Object 150](/wiki/Object_150 "Object 150") (IT-1) Object 167, Object 137Ml, Object 155Ml, [Object 287](/wiki/Taifun_9M15 "Taifun 9M15"), [Object 775](/wiki/Obiekt_775 "Obiekt 775")),

#### Flame tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Flame tank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Flamethrowers_in_Action%2C_August_1944_TR2313.jpg/250px-Flamethrowers_in_Action%2C_August_1944_TR2313.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flamethrowers_in_Action,_August_1944_TR2313.jpg)

British [Churchill Crocodile](/wiki/Churchill_Crocodile "Churchill Crocodile") flame tank

Main article: [Flame tank](/wiki/Flame_tank "Flame tank")

A **flame tank** is an otherwise-standard [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank") equipped with a [flamethrower](/wiki/Flamethrower "Flamethrower"), most commonly used to supplement [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") attacks against [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification"), confined spaces, or other obstacles. The type only reached significant use in the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), during which the United States, [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") and the United Kingdom (including members of the [British Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations")) all produced flamethrower-equipped tanks. Usually, the flame projector replaced one of the tank's machineguns, however, some flame projectors replaced the tank's main gun. Fuel for the flame weapon was generally carried inside the tank, although a few designs mounted the fuel externally, such as the armoured trailer used on the [Churchill Crocodile](/wiki/Churchill_Crocodile "Churchill Crocodile").

Flame tanks have been superseded by [thermobaric weapons](/wiki/Thermobaric_weapon "Thermobaric weapon"), such as the Russian [TOS-1](/wiki/TOS-1 "TOS-1"), which provide greater efficiency and safety.

#### Infantry tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Infantry tank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/MatildaII.jpg/250px-MatildaII.jpg)](/wiki/File:MatildaII.jpg)

A British [Matilda tank](/wiki/Matilda_II "Matilda II") displaying a captured [Italian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") flag

Main article: [Infantry tank](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank")

The idea for this tank was developed during World War I by British and French. The [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") tank was designed to work in concert with infantry in the assault, moving mostly at a walking pace, and carrying heavy armour to survive defensive fire. Its main purpose was to suppress enemy fire, crush obstacles such as barbed-wire entanglements, and served as a shield for infantry on their advance into and through enemy lines by giving mobile [overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch_(military_tactic) "Overwatch (military tactic)") and [cover](/wiki/Suppressive_fire "Suppressive fire"). The French [Renault FT](/wiki/Renault_FT "Renault FT") was the first iteration of this concept. The British and French retained the concept between the wars and into the Second World War era. Because infantry tanks did not need to be fast, they could carry heavy armour. One of the best-known infantry tanks was the [Matilda II](/wiki/Matilda_II "Matilda II") of World War II. Other examples include the French [R-35](/wiki/Renault_R35 "Renault R35"), the British [Valentine](/wiki/Valentine_Tank "Valentine Tank"), and the British [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill_Tank "Churchill Tank").

By the end of World War II the development of faster and more versatile tanks, like the British Centurion, which could perform both Infantry and Cruiser roles, rendered the Infantry tank concept obsolete.

#### Cruiser tank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cruiser tank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/IWM-E-17616-Crusader-19421002.jpg/250px-IWM-E-17616-Crusader-19421002.jpg)](/wiki/File:IWM-E-17616-Crusader-19421002.jpg)

2 pdr-armed [Crusader tank](/wiki/Crusader_tank "Crusader tank") in the desert

Main article: [Cruiser tank](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank")

A cruiser tank, or cavalry tank, was designed to move fast and exploit penetrations of the enemy front. The idea originated in "[Plan 1919](/wiki/Plan_1919 "Plan 1919")", a British plan to break the trench deadlock of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in part via the use of high-speed tanks. The first cruiser tank was the British [Whippet](/wiki/Medium_Mark_A_Whippet "Medium Mark A Whippet"). Between the wars, this concept was implemented in the "fast tanks" pioneered by [J. Walter Christie](/wiki/J._Walter_Christie "J. Walter Christie"). These led to the Soviet [BT tank series](/wiki/BT_Tank "BT Tank") and the British [cruiser tank series](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank").

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), British cruiser tanks were designed to complement [infantry tanks](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank"), exploiting gains made by the latter to attack and disrupt the enemy rear areas. In order to give them the required speed, cruiser designs sacrificed armour and armament compared to the infantry tanks. Pure British cruisers were generally replaced by more capable medium tanks such as the [US Sherman](/wiki/Sherman_Tank "Sherman Tank") and, to a lesser extent, the [Cromwell](/wiki/Cromwell_tank "Cromwell tank") by 1943.

The Soviet fast tank (*bistrokhodniy tank*, or [BT tank](/wiki/BT_tank "BT tank")) classification also came out of the infantry/cavalry concept of armoured warfare and formed the basis for the British cruisers after 1936. The T-34 was a development of this line of tanks as well, though their armament, armour, and all-round capability places them firmly in the medium tank category.

Cruiser tanks struggled against heavily armored German tanks like the Tiger and Panther; this led to a shift in British tank design towards the "universal tank" concept epitomized by the Centurion flame tank.

#### Specialist tanks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Specialist tanks")]

Specialist tanks are armored vehicles tailored for specific roles beyond traditional combat. They include features for tasks such as bridging gaps, clearing mines or amphibious operations.

### Armoured car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Armoured car")]

Main article: [Armored car (military)](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)")

The [armoured car](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") is a wheeled, often lightly armoured, vehicle adapted as a fighting machine. Its earliest form consisted of a motorised ironside chassis fitted with firing ports. By [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), this had evolved into a mobile fortress[[32]](#cite_note-32) equipped with command equipment, searchlights, and machine guns for self-defence. It was soon proposed that the requirements for the armament and layout of armoured cars be somewhat similar to those on naval craft, resulting in turreted vehicles. The first example carried a single revolving cupola with a [Vickers gun](/wiki/Vickers_gun "Vickers gun"); modern armoured cars may boast heavier armament – ranging from twin machine guns to large calibre cannon.

Some multi-axled wheeled fighting vehicles can be quite heavy, and superior to older or smaller tanks in terms of armour and armament. Others are often used in military marches and processions, or for the escorting of important figures. Under peacetime conditions, they form an essential part of most standing armies. Armoured car units can move without the assistance of transporters and cover great distances with fewer logistical problems than tracked vehicles.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), armoured cars were used for reconnaissance alongside scout cars. Their guns were suitable for some defence if they encountered enemy armoured fighting vehicles, but they were not intended to engage enemy [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"). [Armoured cars](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") have since been used in the offensive role against tanks with varying degrees of success, most notably during the [South African Border War](/wiki/South_African_Border_War "South African Border War"), [Toyota War](/wiki/Toyota_War "Toyota War"), the [Invasion of Kuwait](/wiki/Invasion_of_Kuwait "Invasion of Kuwait"), and other [lower-intensity conflicts](/wiki/Low_intensity_conflict "Low intensity conflict").

#### Aerosledge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Aerosledge")]

Main article: [Aerosledge](/wiki/Aerosledge "Aerosledge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/RF-8.jpg/250px-RF-8.jpg)](/wiki/File:RF-8.jpg)

The [RF-8](/wiki/RF-8 "RF-8"), a smaller World War II model, powered by an inexpensive automotive engine

An *aerosledge* is a type of propeller-driven [snowmobile](/wiki/Snowmobile "Snowmobile"), running on skis, used for communications, mail deliveries, medical aid, emergency recovery and border patrolling in northern Russia, as well as for recreation. Aerosledges were used by the Soviet [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") during the [Winter War](/wiki/Winter_War "Winter War") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Some early aerosledges were built by young [Igor Sikorsky](/wiki/Igor_Sikorsky "Igor Sikorsky") in 1909–10, before he built multi-engine airplanes and helicopters. They were very light [plywood](/wiki/Plywood "Plywood") vehicles on skis, propelled by old airplane engines and propellers.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### Scout car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Scout car")]

Main article: [Scout car](/wiki/Scout_car "Scout car")

A *scout car* is a military armoured [reconnaissance vehicle](/wiki/Reconnaissance_vehicle "Reconnaissance vehicle"), capable of off-road mobility and often carrying mounted weapons such as [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") for offensive capabilities and crew protection. They often only carry an operational crew aboard, which differentiates them from wheeled armoured personnel carriers (APCs) and [infantry mobility vehicles](/wiki/Infantry_mobility_vehicle "Infantry mobility vehicle") (IMVs), but early scout cars, such as the open-topped US [M3 scout car](/wiki/M3_scout_car "M3 scout car") could carry a crew of seven. The term is often used synonymously with the more general term armoured car, which also includes armoured civilian vehicles. They are also differentiated by being designed and built for purpose, as opposed to improvised "[technicals](/wiki/Technical_(vehicle) "Technical (vehicle)")" which might serve in the same role.

#### Reconnaissance vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Reconnaissance vehicle")]

Main articles: [Reconnaissance vehicle](/wiki/Reconnaissance_vehicle "Reconnaissance vehicle") and [Armoured reconnaissance](/wiki/Armoured_reconnaissance "Armoured reconnaissance")

A *reconnaissance vehicle*, also known as a *scout vehicle*, is a [military vehicle](/wiki/Military_vehicle "Military vehicle") used for forward [reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance"). Both tracked and wheeled reconnaissance vehicles are in service. In some countries, light tanks such as the [M551 Sheridan](/wiki/M551_Sheridan "M551 Sheridan") and [AMX-13](/wiki/AMX-13 "AMX-13") are also used by scout platoons. Reconnaissance vehicles are usually designed with a low profile or small size and are lightly armoured, relying on speed and cover to escape detection. Their armament ranges from a [medium machine gun](/wiki/Medium_machine_gun "Medium machine gun") to an [autocannon](/wiki/Autocannon "Autocannon"). Modern examples are often fitted with [ATGMs](/wiki/ATGM "ATGM") and a wide range of sensors.

Armoured reconnaissance is the combination of [terrestrial reconnaissance](/wiki/Terrestrial_reconnaissance "Terrestrial reconnaissance") with [armoured warfare](/wiki/Armoured_warfare "Armoured warfare") by using tanks and wheeled or tracked armoured reconnaissance vehicles. While the mission of reconnaissance is to gather intelligence about the enemy with the use of reconnaissance vehicles, armoured reconnaissance adds the ability to fight for information, and to have an effect on and to shape the enemy through the performance of traditional armoured tasks. Some [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") and [infantry mobility vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_mobility_vehicle "Infantry mobility vehicle"), such as the [M113](/wiki/M113 "M113"), [TPz Fuchs](/wiki/TPz_Fuchs "TPz Fuchs"), and [Cadillac Gage Commando](/wiki/Cadillac_Gage_Commando "Cadillac Gage Commando") double in the reconnaissance role.

#### Internal security vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Internal security vehicle")]

Main article: [Internal security vehicle](/wiki/Internal_security_vehicle "Internal security vehicle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Bulgarian_m1117.JPG/250px-Bulgarian_m1117.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bulgarian_m1117.JPG)

An American-made Bulgarian [M1117](/wiki/M1117 "M1117")

An internal security vehicle (ISV), also known as an armoured security vehicle (ASV), is a combat vehicle used for suppressing civilian unrest. Security vehicles are typically armed with a turreted [heavy machine gun](/wiki/Heavy_machine_gun "Heavy machine gun") and auxiliary [medium machine gun](/wiki/Medium_machine_gun "Medium machine gun"). The vehicle is designed to minimize firepower dead space. [Non-lethal](/wiki/Non-lethal_weapon "Non-lethal weapon") [water cannons](/wiki/Water_cannon "Water cannon") and [tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas") cannons can provide suppressive fire in lieu of unnecessary deadly fire.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMargiotta199656,_57-34)

The vehicle must be protected against weapons typical of riots. Protection from [improvised incendiary devices](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail") is achieved though coverage of the air intake and exhaust ports as well as a strong locking mechanism on the fuel opening. Turret and door locks prevent access to the interior of the vehicle by rioters. Vision blocks, ballistic glass and window shutters and outside [surveillance cameras](/wiki/Closed-circuit_television "Closed-circuit television") allow protected observation from within the vehicle. Wheeled 4x4 and 6x6 configurations are typical of security vehicles. Tracked security vehicles are often cumbersome and leave negative political connotations for being perceived as an imperial invading force [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

#### Military light utility vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Military light utility vehicle")]

Main article: [Military light utility vehicle](/wiki/Military_light_utility_vehicle "Military light utility vehicle")

Military light utility vehicles are the lightest weight class of military vehicles. It refers to light [4x4](/wiki/4x4 "4x4") military vehicles with light or no armour and all-terrain mobility. This type of vehicle originated in the first half of the 20th century when horses and other [draft animals](/wiki/Draft_animal "Draft animal") were replaced with mechanization. Light utility vehicles such as the [Willys Jeep](/wiki/Willys_Jeep "Willys Jeep") were frequently mounted with .50-calibre machineguns and other small weapons for [hit-and-run tactics](/wiki/Hit-and-run_tactics "Hit-and-run tactics") in World War II, especially by the [British Special Air Service](/wiki/British_Special_Air_Service "British Special Air Service") who used Jeeps to raid [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") airfields during the [North Africa campaign](/wiki/North_Africa_campaign "North Africa campaign"). After the war, vehicles like the [Toyota Mega Cruiser](/wiki/Toyota_Mega_Cruiser "Toyota Mega Cruiser") and [Humvee](/wiki/Humvee "Humvee") filled this role. In the 21st century, [improvised explosive devices](/wiki/Improvised_explosive_devices "Improvised explosive devices") continue to pose threat to mobile infantry resulting in light utility vehicles being made heavier and with more armour.

#### Improvised fighting vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Improvised fighting vehicle")]

Main articles: [Improvised vehicle armour](/wiki/Improvised_vehicle_armour "Improvised vehicle armour"), [Improvised fighting vehicle](/wiki/Improvised_fighting_vehicle "Improvised fighting vehicle"), [Narco tank](/wiki/Narco_tank "Narco tank"), [Gun truck](/wiki/Gun_truck "Gun truck"), and [Technical (vehicle)](/wiki/Technical_(vehicle) "Technical (vehicle)")

An improvised fighting vehicle is a combat vehicle resulting from modifications to a civilian or military non-combat vehicle in order to give it a fighting capability. Such modifications usually consist of the grafting of [armour plating](/wiki/Improvised_vehicle_armour "Improvised vehicle armour") and [weapon systems](/wiki/Weapon_systems "Weapon systems"). Various militaries have procured such vehicles, ever since the introduction of the first [automobiles](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") into military service.

During the early days, the absence of a doctrine for the military use of automobiles or of an industry dedicated to producing them, lead to much improvisation in the creation of early armoured cars, and other such vehicles. Later, despite the advent of [arms industries](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry") in many countries, several armies still resorted to using ad hoc contraptions, often in response to unexpected military situations, or as a result of the development of new [tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics") for which no available vehicle was suitable. The construction of improvised fighting vehicles may also reflect a lack of means for the force that uses them. This is especially true in [underdeveloped countries](/wiki/Underdeveloped_country "Underdeveloped country") and even in [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"), where various armies and [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") forces have used them, as they are more affordable than military-grade combat vehicles.

Modern examples include **military gun truck** used by units of regular armies or other official government armed forces, based on a conventional [military cargo truck](/wiki/Military_vehicle "Military vehicle"), that is able to carry a large weight of weapons and armour. They have mainly been used by regular armies to escort military convoys in regions subject to ambush by [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") forces. "[Narco tanks](/wiki/Narco_tank "Narco tank")", used by [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") drug cartels in the [Mexican drug war](/wiki/Mexican_drug_war "Mexican drug war"), are built from such trucks, which combines [operational mobility](/wiki/Operational_mobility "Operational mobility"), [tactical](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics") [offensive](/wiki/Offensive_(military) "Offensive (military)"), and [defensive](/wiki/Defense_(military) "Defense (military)") capabilities.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Troop carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Troop carriers")]

Troop-carrying AFVs are divided into three main types – armoured personnel carriers (APCs), infantry fighting vehicles (IFVs) and infantry mobility vehicles (IMV). The main difference between the three is their intended role – the APC is designed purely to transport troops and is armed for self-defence only – whereas the IFV is designed to provide close-quarters and anti-armour fire support to the infantry it carries. IMV is a wheeled armoured personnel carrier serving as a military patrol, reconnaissance or security vehicle.

#### Armoured personnel carrier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Armoured personnel carrier")]

Main article: [Armoured personnel carrier](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier")

Armoured personnel carriers (**APCs**) are intended to carry infantry quickly and relatively safely to the point where they are deployed. In the [Battle of Amiens](/wiki/Battle_of_Amiens_(1918) "Battle of Amiens (1918)"), 8 August 1918, the British [Mk V\* tank](/wiki/Mk_V_tank#Mark_V_series "Mk V tank") (a lengthened Mark V) carried a small number of machine gunners as an experiment, but the men were debilitated by the conditions inside the vehicle.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Later that year the first purpose-built APC, the British [Mk IX tank](/wiki/Mark_IX_tank "Mark IX tank") (Mark Nine), appeared. In 1944, the Canadian general [Guy Simonds](/wiki/Guy_Simonds "Guy Simonds") ordered the conversion of redundant armoured vehicles to carry troops (generically named "[Kangaroos](/wiki/Kangaroo_(armoured_personnel_carrier) "Kangaroo (armoured personnel carrier)")"). This proved highly successful, even without training, and the concept was widely used in the [21st Army Group](/wiki/21st_Army_Group "21st Army Group"). Post-war, specialised designs were built, such as the Soviet [BTR-60](/wiki/BTR-60 "BTR-60") and US [M113](/wiki/M113_armored_personnel_carrier "M113 armored personnel carrier").

#### Infantry fighting vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Infantry fighting vehicle")]

Main article: [Infantry fighting vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle")

An *infantry fighting vehicle* (*IFV*), also known as a *mechanized infantry combat vehicle* (*MICV*), is a type of armoured fighting vehicle used to carry [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") into battle and provide [direct](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") [fire support](/wiki/Fire_support "Fire support").[[40]](#cite_note-McNab-40) The first example of an IFV was the [West German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") [Schützenpanzer Lang HS.30](/wiki/Sch%C3%BCtzenpanzer_Lang_HS.30 "Schützenpanzer Lang HS.30") which served in the [Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr") from 1958 until the early 1980s.

IFVs are similar to armoured personnel carriers (APCs) and [infantry carrier vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") (ICVs), designed to transport a [section](/wiki/Section_(military_unit) "Section (military unit)") or [squad](/wiki/Squad "Squad") of infantry (generally between five and ten men) and their equipment. They are differentiated from APCs – which are purely "troop-transport" vehicles armed only for self-defence – because they are designed to give direct fire support to the dismounted infantry and so usually have significantly enhanced armament. IFVs also often have improved [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour") and some have firing ports (allowing the infantry to fire [personal weapons](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") while mounted).

They are typically armed with an [autocannon](/wiki/Autocannon "Autocannon") of 20 to 57 mm calibre, 7.62mm machine guns, [anti-tank guided missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile") (ATGMs) and/or [surface-to-air missiles](/wiki/Surface-to-air_missile "Surface-to-air missile") (SAMs). IFVs are usually [tracked](/wiki/Caterpillar_track "Caterpillar track"), but some wheeled vehicles fall into this category. IFVs are generally less heavily armed and armoured than [main battle tanks](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"). They sometimes carry anti-tank missiles to protect and support infantry against armoured threats, such as the NATO [TOW](/wiki/BGM-71_TOW "BGM-71 TOW") missile and Soviet [Bastion](/wiki/9M117_Bastion "9M117 Bastion"), which offer a significant threat to tanks. Specially equipped IFVs have taken on some of the roles of light tanks; they are used by reconnaissance organizations, and light IFVs are used by airborne units which must be able to fight without the heavy firepower of tanks.

#### Infantry mobility vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Infantry mobility vehicle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/AMZ_Tur_PICT0096.jpg/250px-AMZ_Tur_PICT0096.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMZ_Tur_PICT0096.jpg)

[Polish](/wiki/Poland "Poland") [AMZ Tur](/wiki/AMZ_Tur "AMZ Tur")

Main articles: [Infantry mobility vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_mobility_vehicle "Infantry mobility vehicle") and [MRAP](/wiki/MRAP "MRAP")

An *infantry mobility vehicle* (*IMV*) or *protected patrol vehicle* (*PPV*) is a wheeled armoured personnel carrier (APC) serving as a military patrol, reconnaissance or security vehicle. Examples include the [ATF Dingo](/wiki/ATF_Dingo "ATF Dingo"), [AMZ Dzik](/wiki/AMZ_Dzik "AMZ Dzik"), [AMZ Tur](/wiki/AMZ_Tur "AMZ Tur"), [Mungo ESK](/wiki/Mungo_ESK "Mungo ESK"), and [Bushmaster IMV](/wiki/Bushmaster_IMV "Bushmaster IMV"). This term also applies to the vehicles currently being fielded as part of the [MRAP](/wiki/MRAP "MRAP") program.

IMVs were developed in response to the threats of modern counterinsurgency warfare, with an emphasis on [Ambush](/wiki/Ambush "Ambush") Protection and [Mine](/wiki/Improvised_Explosive_Device "Improvised Explosive Device")-Resistance. Similar vehicles existed long before the term IMV was coined, such as the French [VAB](/wiki/V%C3%A9hicule_de_l%27Avant_Blind%C3%A9 "Véhicule de l'Avant Blindé") and South African [Buffel](/wiki/Buffel "Buffel"). The term is coming more into use to differentiate light 4x4 wheeled APCs from the traditional 8x8 wheeled APCs. It is a [neologism](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") for what might have been classified in the past as an armoured [scout car](/wiki/Scout_car "Scout car"), such as the [BRDM](/wiki/BRDM "BRDM"), but the IMV is distinguished by having a requirement to carry dismountable infantry. The up-armoured [M1114](/wiki/M1114 "M1114") Humvee variant can be seen as an adaptation of the unarmoured Humvee to serve in the IMV role.

### Amphibious vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Amphibious vehicles")]

Main articles: [Amphibious vehicle § Armored](/wiki/Amphibious_vehicle#Armored "Amphibious vehicle"), [Amphibious warfare](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare "Amphibious warfare"), [amphibious assault](/wiki/Amphibious_assault "Amphibious assault"), and [Landing craft](/wiki/Landing_craft "Landing craft")

Many modern military vehicles, ranging from light wheeled command and reconnaissance, through armoured personnel carriers and tanks, are manufactured with amphibious capabilities. Contemporary wheeled armoured amphibians include the French [Véhicule de l'Avant Blindé](/wiki/V%C3%A9hicule_de_l%27Avant_Blind%C3%A9 "Véhicule de l'Avant Blindé") and [Véhicule Blindé Léger](/wiki/V%C3%A9hicule_Blind%C3%A9_L%C3%A9ger "Véhicule Blindé Léger"). The latter is a small, lightly armoured [4×4](/wiki/4%C3%974 "4×4") all-terrain vehicle that is fully amphibious and can swim at 5.4 km/h. The VAB (*Véhicule de l'Avant Blindé* – 'armoured vanguard vehicle') is a fully amphibious armoured personnel carrier powered in the water by two water jets, that entered service in 1976 and produced in numerous configurations, ranging from basic personnel carrier, anti-tank missile platform.

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") the [Soviet bloc](/wiki/Soviet_bloc "Soviet bloc") states developed a number of amphibious APCs, fighting vehicles and tanks, both wheeled and tracked. Most of the vehicles the Soviets designed were amphibious, or could ford deep water. Wheeled examples are the [BRDM-1](/wiki/BRDM-1 "BRDM-1") and [BRDM-2](/wiki/BRDM-2 "BRDM-2") [4x4](/wiki/4x4 "4x4") armoured scout cars, as well as the [BTR-60](/wiki/BTR-60 "BTR-60"), [BTR-70](/wiki/BTR-70 "BTR-70"), [BTR-80](/wiki/BTR-80 "BTR-80"), [BTR-94](/wiki/BTR-94 "BTR-94") and [BTR-90](/wiki/BTR-90 "BTR-90") [8x8](/wiki/Eight-wheel_drive "Eight-wheel drive") armoured personnel carriers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Type_2_Ka-Mi_amphibious_tank.jpg/250px-Type_2_Ka-Mi_amphibious_tank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Type_2_Ka-Mi_amphibious_tank.jpg)

[Type 2 Ka-Mi](/wiki/Type_2_Ka-Mi "Type 2 Ka-Mi") tank with flotation sections attached

During the 1930s and 1940s, Japan produced a number of amphibious tank designs, including prototypes such as the [Sumida amphibious armored car](/wiki/Sumida_amphibious_armored_car "Sumida amphibious armored car") (AMP), [SR I-Go](/wiki/SR_I-Go "SR I-Go"), [SR II Ro-Go](/wiki/SR_II_Ro-Go "SR II Ro-Go"), [SR III Ha-Go](/wiki/SR_III_Ha-Go "SR III Ha-Go"), [Type 1 Mi-Sha](/wiki/Type_2_Ka-Mi#History_and_development "Type 2 Ka-Mi") (a/k/a Type 1 Ka-Mi) and [Type 5 To-Ku](/wiki/Type_5_To-Ku "Type 5 To-Ku").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomczyk20033,_4,_32-41) Production amphibious tanks during World War II included the [Type 2 Ka-Mi](/wiki/Type_2_Ka-Mi "Type 2 Ka-Mi"), and [Type 3 Ka-Chi](/wiki/Type_3_Ka-Chi "Type 3 Ka-Chi"); production amphibious transports included the [F B swamp vehicle](/wiki/F_B_swamp_vehicle "F B swamp vehicle") and [Type 4 Ka-Tsu](/wiki/Type_4_Ka-Tsu "Type 4 Ka-Tsu") APC. All production units were for use by the [Japanese Special Naval Landing Forces](/wiki/Japanese_Special_Naval_Landing_Forces "Japanese Special Naval Landing Forces") in campaigns in the Pacific with amphibious operations.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomczyk20034-42)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga200723,_24-43)

The United States started developing a long line of [Landing Vehicle Tracked](/wiki/Landing_Vehicle_Tracked "Landing Vehicle Tracked") (LVT) designs from c. 1940. The US Marine Corps currently uses the AAV7-A1 [Assault Amphibious Vehicle](/wiki/Assault_Amphibious_Vehicle "Assault Amphibious Vehicle"), which was to be succeeded by the [Expeditionary Fighting Vehicle](/wiki/Expeditionary_Fighting_Vehicle "Expeditionary Fighting Vehicle"), which was capable of planing on water and can achieve water speeds of 37–46 km/h. The EFV project has been cancelled.

A significant number of tracked armoured vehicles that are primarily intended for land-use, have some amphibious capability, tactically useful inland, reducing dependence on bridges. They use their tracks, sometimes with added propeller or water jets for propulsion. As long as the banks have a shallow enough slopes to enter or leave the water they can cross rivers and water obstacles.

Some heavy tanks can operate amphibiously with a fabric skirt to add [buoyancy](/wiki/Buoyancy "Buoyancy"). The [Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") [DD tank](/wiki/DD_tank "DD tank") used in the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") had this setup. When in water the waterproof float screen was raised and propellers deployed. Some modern vehicles use a similar skirt.
