### Airborne vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Airborne vehicles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/C-130_airdrop.jpg/250px-C-130_airdrop.jpg)](/wiki/File:C-130_airdrop.jpg)

[C-130](/wiki/C-130 "C-130") airdrops an [M551](/wiki/M551 "M551") light tank

Lightweight armoured fighting vehicles designed or modified to be [carried by aircraft](/wiki/Airlift "Airlift") and delivered by air drop, helicopter lift, glider, or air landing with infantry to provide heavier tactical firepower and mobility. The air-equivalent to amphibious vehicles, the main advantage of airborne forces is their ability to be deployed into combat zones without land passage, as long as the [airspace](/wiki/Airspace "Airspace") is accessible. Airborne vehicles are limited only by the tonnage capacity of their [transport aircraft](/wiki/Military_transport_aircraft "Military transport aircraft"). Airborne vehicles typically lack the armour and supplies necessary for prolonged combat, so they are utilized for establishing an [airhead](/wiki/Airhead_(warfare) "Airhead (warfare)") to bring in larger forces before carrying out other combat objectives. One modern example is the German [Wiesel AWC](/wiki/Wiesel_AWC "Wiesel AWC"). The USA also created the [M22 Locust](/wiki/M22_Locust "M22 Locust") as a way to aid paratroopers/ being paradropped in as it was very lightly armoured and very small.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Armoured engineering vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Armoured engineering vehicle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/IDF_Puma_CEV_%285%29.jpg/250px-IDF_Puma_CEV_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF_Puma_CEV_(5).jpg)

[IDF Puma](/wiki/IDF_Puma "IDF Puma") combat engineering vehicle

Main article: [Military engineering vehicle](/wiki/Military_engineering_vehicle "Military engineering vehicle")

Modern engineering AFV's utilize chassis based on main battle tank platforms: these vehicles are as well armoured and protected as tanks, designed to keep up with tanks, breach obstacles to help tanks get to wherever it needs to be, perform utility functions necessary to expedite mission objectives of tanks, and to conduct other earth-moving and engineering work on the battlefield. These vehicles go by different names depending upon the country of use or manufacture. In the United States the term "combat engineer vehicle (CEV)" is used, in the United Kingdom the term "[Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers](/wiki/Armoured_Vehicle_Royal_Engineers "Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers") (AVRE)" is used, while in Canada and other commonwealth nations the term "armoured engineer vehicle (AEV)" is used. There is no set template for what such a vehicle will look like, yet likely features include a large dozer blade or mine ploughs, a large calibre demolition cannon, augers, winches, excavator arms and cranes, or lifting booms.

Although the term "armoured engineer vehicle" is used specifically to describe these multi-purpose tank-based engineering vehicles, that term is also used more generically in British and Commonwealth militaries to describe all heavy tank-based engineering vehicles used in the support of mechanized forces. Thus, "armoured engineer vehicle" used generically would refer to AEV, AVLB, Assault Breachers, and so on. Good examples of this type of vehicle include the UK [Trojan AVRE](/wiki/Trojan_Armoured_Vehicle_Royal_Engineers "Trojan Armoured Vehicle Royal Engineers"), the Russian IMR, and the US [M728 Combat Engineer Vehicle](/wiki/M728_Combat_Engineer_Vehicle "M728 Combat Engineer Vehicle").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/IL%C3%9C_2012_-_Panzer_Keiler.jpg/250px-IL%C3%9C_2012_-_Panzer_Keiler.jpg)](/wiki/File:IL%C3%9C_2012_-_Panzer_Keiler.jpg)

A [German army](/wiki/German_Army "German Army") [Rheinmetall](/wiki/Rheinmetall "Rheinmetall") [Keiler](/wiki/Keiler_(mine_flail) "Keiler (mine flail)"). It uses a heavy-duty rotor-powered mine flail, which causes mines it comes in contact with to safely detonate.

#### Breaching vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Breaching vehicle")]

Main article: [Military engineering vehicle § Breaching vehicle](/wiki/Military_engineering_vehicle#Breaching_vehicle "Military engineering vehicle")

A **breaching vehicle** is especially designed to clear pathways for troops and other vehicles through [minefields](/wiki/Minefields "Minefields") and along [roadside bombs](/wiki/Roadside_bomb "Roadside bomb") and other [improvised explosive devices](/wiki/Improvised_Explosive_Device "Improvised Explosive Device"). These vehicles are equipped with mechanical or other means for the breaching of man-made obstacles. Common types of breaching vehicles include mechanical [flails](/wiki/Mine_flail "Mine flail"), mine plough vehicles, and mine roller vehicles.

#### Armoured bulldozer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Armoured bulldozer")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/IDF-D9-Zachi-Evenor-001.jpg/250px-IDF-D9-Zachi-Evenor-001.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF-D9-Zachi-Evenor-001.jpg)

[IDF Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9") [armoured bulldozer](/wiki/Armoured_bulldozer "Armoured bulldozer")

Main article: [Armored bulldozer](/wiki/Armored_bulldozer "Armored bulldozer")

The armoured bulldozer is a basic tool of [combat engineering](/wiki/Combat_engineering "Combat engineering"). These [combat engineering vehicles](/wiki/Combat_engineering_vehicle "Combat engineering vehicle") combine the earth moving capabilities of the bulldozer with armour which protects the vehicle and its operator in or near combat. Most are civilian bulldozers modified by addition of [vehicle armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour")/military equipment, but some are [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") stripped of armament and fitted with a dozer blade. Some tanks have bulldozer blades while retaining their armament, but this does not make them armoured bulldozers as such, because combat remains the primary role – earth moving is a secondary task.

#### Armoured recovery vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Armoured recovery vehicle")]

Main article: [Armoured recovery vehicle](/wiki/Armoured_recovery_vehicle "Armoured recovery vehicle")

An *armoured recovery vehicle* (*ARV*) is a type of [vehicle recovery](/wiki/Vehicle_recovery_(military) "Vehicle recovery (military)") armoured fighting vehicle used to repair battle- or mine-damaged as well as broken-down armoured vehicles during combat, or to tow them out of the danger zone for more extensive repairs. To this end the term *armoured repair and recovery vehicle* (*ARRV*) is also used.

ARVs are normally built on the [chassis](/wiki/Chassis "Chassis") of a main battle tank (MBT), but some are also constructed on the basis of other armoured fighting vehicles, mostly armoured personnel carriers (APCs). ARVs are usually built on the basis of a vehicle in the same class as they are supposed to recover; a tank-based ARV is used to recover tanks, while an APC-based one recovers APCs, but does not have the power to tow a much heavier tank.

#### Armoured vehicle-launched bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Armoured vehicle-launched bridge")]

Main article: [Armoured vehicle-launched bridge](/wiki/Armoured_vehicle-launched_bridge "Armoured vehicle-launched bridge")

An *armoured vehicle-launched bridge* (*AVLB*) is a combat support vehicle, sometimes regarded as a subtype of [combat engineering vehicle](/wiki/Combat_engineering_vehicle "Combat engineering vehicle"), designed to assist militaries in rapidly deploying [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") and other armoured fighting vehicles across rivers. The AVLB is usually a tracked vehicle converted from a tank chassis to carry a folding metal bridge instead of weapons. The AVLB's job is to allow armoured or infantry units to cross water, when a river too deep for vehicles to wade through is reached, and no bridge is conveniently located (or sufficiently sturdy, a substantial concern when moving 60-ton tanks).

The bridge layer unfolds and launches its cargo, providing a ready-made bridge across the obstacle in only minutes. Once the span has been put in place, the AVLB vehicle detaches from the bridge, and moves aside to allow traffic to pass. Once all of the vehicles have crossed, it crosses the bridge itself and reattaches to the bridge on the other side. It then retracts the span ready to move off again. A similar procedure can be employed to allow crossings of small chasms or similar obstructions. AVLBs can carry bridges of 60 feet (18 metres) or greater in length. By using a tank chassis, the bridge layer is able to cover the same terrain as main battle tanks, and the provision of armour allows them to operate even in the face of enemy fire. However, this is not a universal attribute: some exceptionally sturdy 6x6 or 8x8 truck chassis have lent themselves to bridge-layer applications.

#### Combat engineer section carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Combat engineer section carriers")]

*Combat engineer section carriers* are used to transport [sappers](/wiki/Sapper "Sapper") ([combat engineers](/wiki/Combat_engineer "Combat engineer")) and can be fitted with [bulldozers](/wiki/Bulldozer "Bulldozer")' blades and other mine-breaching devices. They are often used as APCs because of their carrying ability and heavy protection. They are usually armed with [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") and grenade launchers and usually tracked to provide enough tractive force to push blades and rakes. Some examples are the U.S. [M113 APC](/wiki/M113_Armored_Personnel_Carrier "M113 Armored Personnel Carrier"), [IDF Puma](/wiki/IDF_Puma "IDF Puma"), [Nagmachon](/wiki/Nagmachon "Nagmachon"), Husky, and U.S. [M1132 ESV](/wiki/M1132_Engineer_Support_Vehicle "M1132 Engineer Support Vehicle") (a [Stryker](/wiki/Stryker "Stryker") variant).

### Air defence vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Air defence vehicles")]

Main articles: [Self-propelled anti-aircraft weapon](/wiki/Self-propelled_anti-aircraft_weapon "Self-propelled anti-aircraft weapon"); [Counter rocket, artillery, and mortar](/wiki/Counter_rocket,_artillery,_and_mortar "Counter rocket, artillery, and mortar"); and [Close-in weapon system](/wiki/Close-in_weapon_system "Close-in weapon system")

An anti-aircraft vehicle, also known as a self-propelled anti-aircraft gun (SPAAG) or self-propelled air defense system (SPAD), is a mobile vehicle with a dedicated anti-aircraft capability.
Specific weapon systems used include [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_guns "Machine guns"), [anti-aircraft autocannons](/wiki/Autocannon "Autocannon"), larger anti-air guns, or [surface-to-air-missiles](/wiki/Surface-to-air-missiles "Surface-to-air-missiles"), and some mount both guns and longer-ranged missiles (e.g. the Pantsir-S1). Platforms used include both trucks and heavier combat vehicles such as armored personnel carriers and tanks, which add protection from aircraft, artillery, and small arms fire for front line deployment.
Anti-aircraft guns are usually mounted in a quickly-traversing turret with a high rate of elevation, for tracking fast-moving aircraft. They are often in dual or quadruple mounts, allowing a high rate of fire. In addition, most anti-aircraft guns can be used in a direct-fire role against surface targets to great effect. In the early 21st century, missiles (generally mounted on similar turrets) largely supplanted anti-aircraft guns, though guns have recently shown revived utility against slow, low-flying drones.

### Self-propelled artillery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Self-propelled artillery")]

Main articles: [Self-propelled artillery](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery") and [Self-propelled gun](/wiki/Self-propelled_gun "Self-propelled gun")

Self-propelled [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") vehicles give mobility to [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"). Within the term are covered [self-propelled guns](/wiki/Self-propelled_gun "Self-propelled gun") (or [howitzers](/wiki/Howitzer "Howitzer")) and [rocket artillery](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery"). They are highly mobile, usually based on tracked chassis carrying either a large howitzer or other field gun or alternatively a [mortar](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") or some form of rocket or missile launcher. They are usually used for long-range [indirect](/wiki/Indirect-fire "Indirect-fire") bombardment support on the battlefield.

In the past, self-propelled artillery has included direct-fire "Gun Motor Carriage" vehicles, such as [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun") and [tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer") (also known as self-propelled anti-tank guns). These have been heavily armoured vehicles, the former providing danger-close fire-support for infantry and the latter acting as specialized anti-tank vehicles.

Modern self-propelled artillery vehicles may superficially resemble tanks, but they are generally lightly armoured, too lightly to survive in direct-fire combat. However, they protect their crews against [shrapnel](/wiki/Shrapnel_(fragment) "Shrapnel (fragment)") and [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms") and are therefore usually included as armoured fighting vehicles. Many are equipped with [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") for defence against enemy infantry.

The key advantage of self-propelled over towed artillery is that it can be brought into action much faster. Before towed artillery can be used, it has to stop, unlimber and the guns set up. To move position, the guns must be limbered up again and brought – usually towed – to the new location. By comparison, self-propelled artillery in combination with modern communications, can stop at a chosen location and begin firing almost immediately, then quickly move on to a new position. This ability is very useful in a mobile conflict and particularly on the advance.

Conversely, towed artillery was and remains cheaper to build and maintain. It is also lighter and can be taken to places that self-propelled guns cannot reach, so despite the advantages of the self-propelled artillery, towed guns remain in the [arsenals](/wiki/Arsenal "Arsenal") of many modern armies.

#### Assault gun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Assault gun")]

Main article: [Assault gun](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun")

An assault gun is a gun or howitzer mounted on a motor vehicle or armoured chassis, designed for use in the [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") role in support of [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") when attacking other infantry or [fortified](/wiki/Fortified "Fortified") positions.

Historically, the custom-built fully armoured assault guns usually mounted the gun or howitzer in a fully enclosed [casemate](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate") on a tank chassis. The use of a [casemate](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate") instead of a [gun turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") limited these weapons' [field of fire](/wiki/Field_of_fire_(weaponry) "Field of fire (weaponry)"), but allowed a larger gun to be fitted relative to the chassis, more armour to be fitted for the same weight, and provided a cheaper construction. In most cases, these turretless vehicles also presented a lower profile as a target for the enemy.

#### Mortar carrier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Mortar carrier")]

Main article: [Mortar carrier](/wiki/Mortar_carrier "Mortar carrier")

A mortar carrier, or self-propelled mortar, is a [self-propelled artillery](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery") vehicle carrying one or more [mortar](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") as its primary weapon. Mortar carriers cannot be fired while on the move and some must be dismounted to fire. In U.S. Army doctrine, mortar carriers provide close and immediate indirect fire support for maneuver units while allowing for rapid displacement and quick reaction to the tactical situation. The ability to relocate not only allows fire support to be provided where it is needed faster, but also allows these units to avoid [counter-battery fire](/wiki/Counter-battery_fire "Counter-battery fire"). Mortar carriers have traditionally avoided direct contact with the enemy. Many units report never using secondary weapons in combat.

Prior to the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), American 120 mm mortar platoons reorganized from six [M1064 mortar carriers](/wiki/M1064_mortar_carrier "M1064 mortar carrier") and two M577 fire direction centres (FDC) to four M1064 and one FDC.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The urban environment of Iraq made it difficult to utilize mortars. New technologies such as mortar ballistic computers and communication equipment and are being integrated. Modern era combat is becoming more reliant on [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") support from mortar carrier [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun").

#### Multiple rocket launcher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Multiple rocket launcher")]

Main article: [Multiple rocket launcher](/wiki/Multiple_rocket_launcher "Multiple rocket launcher")

A multiple rocket launcher is a type of unguided [rocket artillery](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery") system. Like other [rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(weapon) "Rocket (weapon)") artillery, multiple rocket launchers are less accurate and have a much lower (sustained) rate of fire than batteries of traditional [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") guns. However, they have the capability of simultaneously dropping many hundreds of kilograms of explosive, with devastating effect.

The [Korean](/wiki/Korea "Korea") [Hwacha](/wiki/Hwacha "Hwacha") is an example of an early weapon system with a resemblance to the modern-day multiple rocket launcher. The first self-propelled multiple rocket launchers – and arguably the most famous – were the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [BM-13 Katyushas](/wiki/Katyusha_rocket_launcher "Katyusha rocket launcher"), first used during World War II and exported to Soviet allies afterwards. They were simple systems in which a rack of launch rails was mounted on the back of a truck. This set the template for modern multiple rocket launchers. The first modern multiple rocket launcher was the [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") *[15 cm Nebelwerfer 41](/wiki/15_cm_Nebelwerfer_41 "15 cm Nebelwerfer 41")* of the 1930s,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a small towed artillery piece. Only later in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") did the British deploy similar weapons in the form of the [Land Mattress](/wiki/Mattress_(rocket) "Mattress (rocket)").The Americans mounted tubular launchers atop [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") tanks to create the [T34 Calliope](/wiki/T34_Calliope "T34 Calliope") rocket launching tank, only used in small numbers, as their closest equivalent to the Katyusha.

#### Missile vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Missile vehicle")]

[Missile vehicles](/wiki/Missile_vehicle "Missile vehicle") are trucks or tractor units designed to carry rockets or missiles. The missile vehicle may be a self-propelled unit, or the missile holder/launcher may be on a trailer towed by a prime mover. They are used in the military forces of a number of countries in the world. Long missiles are commonly transported parallel to the ground on these vehicles, but elevated into an inclined or vertical position for launching.

* A [Transporter erector launcher](/wiki/Transporter_erector_launcher "Transporter erector launcher") (TEL) is a missile vehicle with an integrated prime mover (tractor unit) that can carry, elevate to firing position and launch one or more missiles. Such vehicles exist for both surface-to-air missiles and surface-to-surface missiles.

#### Tank destroyer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Tank destroyer")]

Main articles: [Tank destroyer](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"), [Anti-tank missile carrier](/wiki/Anti-tank_missile_carrier "Anti-tank missile carrier"), and [Anti-tank warfare](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare")

Tank destroyers and tank hunters are armed with an [anti-tank gun](/wiki/Anti-tank_gun "Anti-tank gun") or [anti-tank missile](/wiki/Anti-tank_missile "Anti-tank missile") launcher, and are designed specifically to engage enemy armoured vehicles. Many have been based on a tracked tank chassis, while others are wheeled. Since World War II, main battle tanks have largely replaced gun-armed tank destroyers; although lightly armoured anti-tank guided missile (ATGM) carriers are commonly used for supplementary long-range anti-tank engagements.

In post-[Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") conflict, the resurgence of [expeditionary warfare](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") has seen the emergence of gun-armed wheeled vehicles, sometimes called "protected gun systems", which may bear a superficial resemblance to tank destroyers, but are employed as direct fire support units typically providing support in low intensity operations such as Iraq and Afghanistan. These have the advantage of easier deployment, as only the largest air transports can carry a main battle tank, and their smaller size makes them more effective in urban combat.

Many forces' IFVs carry anti-tank missiles in every infantry platoon, and attack helicopters have also added anti-tank capability to the modern battlefield. But there are still dedicated anti-tank vehicles with very heavy long-range missiles, or intended for airborne use. There have also been dedicated anti-tank vehicles built on ordinary armoured personnel carrier or armoured car chassis. Examples include the U.S. M901 ITV (Improved TOW Vehicle) and the Norwegian NM142, both on an [M113](/wiki/M113 "M113") chassis, several Soviet ATGM launchers based on the [BRDM scout car](/wiki/BRDM-2 "BRDM-2"), the British FV438 [Swingfire](/wiki/Swingfire "Swingfire") and [FV102 Striker](/wiki/FV102_Striker "FV102 Striker") and the [German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") *Raketenjagdpanzer* series built on the chassis of the [HS 30](/wiki/HS_30 "HS 30") and [Marder IFVs](/wiki/Marder_(infantry_fighting_vehicle) "Marder (infantry fighting vehicle)").

### Armoured train

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Armoured_fighting_vehicle&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Armoured train")]

Main articles: [Armoured train](/wiki/Armoured_train "Armoured train") and [Draisine § Military use](/wiki/Draisine#Military_use "Draisine")

An *armoured train* is a railway train protected with [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour"). They are usually equipped with rail cars armed with [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"), [autocannons](/wiki/Autocannon "Autocannon"), [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun"), tank [turrets](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") and [anti-aircraft guns](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_gun "Anti-aircraft gun"). They were mostly used during the late 19th to mid-20th century, when they offered an innovative way to quickly move large amounts of firepower. Their use was discontinued in most countries when road vehicles became much more powerful and offered more flexibility, and because armoured trains were too vulnerable to track sabotage and attacks from the air. However, the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation") used improvised armoured trains in the [Second Chechen War](/wiki/Second_Chechen_War "Second Chechen War") in the late 1990s and 2000s. Armoured trains carrying ballistic missiles have also been used.

The rail cars on an armoured train were designed for many tasks, such as carrying artillery and machine guns, infantry units, anti-tank and anti-aircraft guns. During World War II, the Germans would sometimes put a *Fremdgerät* (captured AFVs such as the French [Somua S-35](/wiki/Somua_S-35 "Somua S-35") or Czech [PzKpfw 38(t)](/wiki/PzKpfw_38(t) "PzKpfw 38(t)")), or obsolescent [Panzer II](/wiki/Panzer_II "Panzer II") light tanks on a flatbed rail car, which could quickly be offloaded by means of a ramp and used away from the railway line to chase down enemy [partisans](/wiki/Partisan_(military) "Partisan (military)").

Different types of armour were used to protect armoured trains from attack. In addition to various metal plates, concrete and sandbags were used in some cases on armoured trains.

Armoured trains were sometimes escorted by a kind of rail-tank called a [draisine](/wiki/Draisine "Draisine"). One such example was the Italian 'Littorina' armoured trolley, which had a cab in the front and rear, each with a control set so it could be driven down the tracks in either direction. Littorina mounted two dual 7.92mm [MG13](/wiki/MG-13 "MG-13") machine gun turrets from [Panzer I](/wiki/Panzer_I "Panzer I") light tanks.
